evidence: Action and namespace tokens are duplicated across package boundaries: `actions.go` defines `NamespaceUpload/NamespaceC2C` and `ActionUploadOpenEndpoint/ActionUploadReset/ActionC2CConnect`, while `contracts.go` repeats equivalent literals in `ActionNamespace*` and `ActionContractBindings`.
`contracts.go` runs `ValidateActionContractBindings()` in `init()` and panics on mismatch, creating import-time failure behavior for any consumer of the package.

`actions.go` builds global registries (`uploadActionContracts`, `c2cActionContracts`, `actionContractsByNamespace`) at package load and can panic in `buildActionContractSlice` if descriptors are missing.

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go defines many embedded function-bag structs (`Runtime*Ops`) and default builders, while goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_docker.go re-declares overlapping wiring logic in `newDockerRuntime`, `urbitRuntimeFromConfig`, and `wireguardRuntimeFromConfig` instead of using one composition path.

goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go and goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go both use large nil-checked callback bags with manual merge/override flows (`withDefaults` and `defaultRuntime*`) that add orchestration layers without reducing behavior branching.

goseg/importer/importer.go mixes package-level mutable callback vars with an `importerRuntime` callback bag, and goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go adds another callback-bag runtime with panic-on-missing behavior; seams are inconsistent and increase indirection for the same dependency-injection goal.

goseg/docker/orchestration/orchestration.go repeats a large wrapper pattern where package-level functions validate inputs then forward to nearly identical runtime methods (e.g., StartContainer/CreateContainer/StopContainerByName/RestartContainer/FindContainer/GetContainerRunningStatus).

goseg/auth/auth.go similarly acts as a broad pass-through facade to session/authsession/tokens with many one-line forwarding functions plus local validation, creating a second orchestration surface without distinct policy boundaries.

goseg/system/wifi.go, goseg/system/system.go, and goseg/system/disk.go combine mutable package globals, process execution, lifecycle control, and domain operations in the same package-level surface; tests compensate with seam-reset helpers (e.g., goseg/system/wifi_test.go testWifiRuntime, goseg/system/disk_test.go resetDiskSeams).
goseg/config/config_update_schema.go uses a very large hand-written field registry with repetitive parse/has/apply closures, signaling a scaling design pattern that is difficult to evolve safely.

findings.summary: Protocol action identity is defined in both packages, creating split authority and drift-prone boundaries.
evidence: `actions.go` defines namespace and action enums (`Namespace*`, `Action*`) while `contracts.go` independently defines `ActionNamespace*` plus string actions in `ActionContractBindings`.
The two packages must stay manually synchronized for the same action surface (`upload/open-endpoint`, `upload/reset`, `c2c/connect`) but no single canonical type is shared.
findings.suggestion: Establish a single protocol authority package for namespace/action identifiers and make both `actions` and `contracts` consume those shared typed constants; remove duplicated string literals and duplicate enums.

findings.summary: Contract registry validation is executed at import time, coupling module load order to runtime availability.
evidence: `contracts.go` `init()` calls `ValidateActionContractBindings()` and panics on any invalid binding.
`actions.go` imports `contracts` and therefore inherits this side effect even when a caller may only need action parsing/metadata access.
findings.suggestion: Move validation out of `init()` into explicit startup/bootstrap validation so failure policy is controlled by the composition root and package imports remain side-effect free.

findings.summary: Action registry construction in globals introduces hidden import-time coupling and panic paths across modules.
evidence: `actions.go` initializes `uploadActionContracts`/`c2cActionContracts`/`actionContractsByNamespace` at package load via contract lookups.
`buildActionContractSlice` panics when a descriptor is absent, so package import can fail based on registry consistency instead of explicit initialization flow.
findings.suggestion: Replace eager global construction with explicit lazy/bootstrapped registry initialization that returns errors; keep exported read APIs but decouple package import from cross-package data validation.

findings.summary: Runtime dependency seams are duplicated across orchestration layers, increasing indirection without new policy value.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines a broad `Runtime*Ops` abstraction tree and default constructors (`defaultRuntimeContainerOps`, `defaultRuntimeUrbit`, `defaultRuntimeSnapshot`).
`runtime_docker.go` repeats overlapping dependency wiring in `newDockerRuntime`, `urbitRuntimeFromConfig`, and `wireguardRuntimeFromConfig` instead of composing directly from the same defaults.
Methods like `WireguardRuntime.WriteWgConf` still perform repeated nil-guard checks at call time, indicating the abstraction does not guarantee required capabilities once constructed.
findings.suggestion: Unify orchestration runtime construction behind one builder path that materializes validated capability groups once (e.g., container, config, filesystem, wireguard), and remove duplicate per-runtime wiring in `runtime_docker.go` by composing from that builder.

findings.summary: Modules mix global mutable seams and runtime structs, obscuring true dependency boundaries.
evidence: `importer.go` uses package-level function variables (`shipworkflowWaitForBootCodeFn`, `dockerDeleteContainerFn`, etc.) alongside `importerRuntime` callback fields for the same unit.
`collectors.go` uses a separate runtime bag plus package globals (`startramServicesRetriever`) and panics for missing callbacks, creating a different contract style from importer.
`wifi.go` has singleton-style globals (`defaultWiFiService`, `defaultWiFiRadio`) while also exposing `wifiRuntime` overrides, introducing another seam strategy in the same codebase.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one DI style per subsystem (prefer explicit runtime structs passed into constructors) and remove package-level mutable callback globals; keep globals only for immutable constants/state and inject behavior via typed runtime dependencies.

findings.summary: Function-bag abstractions expose optional callbacks that fail late, weakening interface honesty.
evidence: `startup_orchestrator.go` builds startup steps from descriptors and handles missing callbacks dynamically (`requiredInitCallback`, `runStartupSubsystem`), so contract validity is deferred to execution time.

`wireguard.go` checks for missing runtime callbacks (`BasePathFn`, `GetLatestContainerInfoFn`, `ReadFileFn`, `CopyFileToVolumeFn`) inside operational methods, producing runtime errors rather than constructor-time validation.

`importer.go` has `ensureInitializedForInit`/`ensureForWorkflow` guards and panic wrappers (`swapSettings`, `startramSettings`), which indicates the runtime type itself does not encode required vs optional capabilities.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bags into smaller role-specific interfaces/structs with constructor-time validation and explicit required fields, so operation methods can assume valid contracts and remove repeated nil checks and panic fallbacks.

findings.summary: Protocol action contract truth is split across docs, action constants, and string-based bindings.
evidence: `README.md` defines contract governance expectations for `actions.go` and `contracts.go`, but runtime bindings in `contracts.go` still use free-form action strings (`open-endpoint`, `reset`, `connect`).
`actions.go` declares the same action vocabulary as typed constants, but `contracts.go` does not consume those constants and cannot be compiler-checked against them.
This creates a boundary where docs can claim a single governance surface while implementation remains multi-source and drift-prone.
findings.suggestion: Establish one canonical action-contract registry package (or generated artifact) that owns namespace/action IDs and descriptors, then have both `actions.go` and `contracts.go` consume that shared definition; update README governance text to point to that single source.

findings.summary: Two parallel namespace type systems leak across module boundaries via string translation.
evidence: `actions.go` defines `type Namespace string` with `NamespaceUpload`/`NamespaceC2C`.
`contracts.go` defines `type ActionNamespace string` with the same semantic values.
`buildActionContractSlice` must convert namespace types using `string(namespace)` to query contract bindings, indicating a boundary model split instead of a shared contract type.
findings.suggestion: Unify namespace ownership into a shared domain type used by both modules, or invert dependency so action metadata is derived directly from contract bindings without intermediate string conversions.

findings.summary: Contract registry validation at package init introduces global startup coupling and panic blast radius.
evidence: `contracts.go` runs `ValidateActionContractBindings()` in `init()` and panics on failure.
`actions.go` also panics during metadata build if descriptor lookup fails (`panic(fmt.Sprintf("missing contract descriptor ..."))`).
Given README marks these files as governed contract surfaces, any partial change can cause import-time panics before application-level error handling or quality gates run.
findings.suggestion: Move registry validation from import-time panic to explicit startup validation with typed errors (or test-only hard-fail), and require CI/runtime bootstrap to call that validator so failures are controlled and diagnosable without global init fragility.

findings.summary: Multiple modules expose large pass-through API layers that duplicate orchestration behavior instead of isolating a clear policy boundary.
evidence: orchestration.go defines many paired functions (`X(...)` and `(*orchestrationRuntime).X(...)`) with near-identical delegation after minimal argument checks.

auth.go repeats the same facade style across token/session functions (`AuthToken`, `CreateToken`, `CheckToken`, `ValidateAndAuthorizeRequestToken`, etc.) by forwarding into `tokens`/`session` packages.
runtime_core.go adds another layer of dependency projection (`NewDockerTransitionRuntime`, `NewDockerHealthRuntime`) that mostly copies function fields from a broader runtime.
findings.suggestion: Collapse pass-through surfaces into smaller capability-focused services: keep one exported boundary per domain (auth runtime, orchestration runtime), move shared validation into reusable helpers/interceptors, and remove duplicated package-level vs method-level wrappers unless they add distinct policy.

findings.summary: System modules mix lifecycle state management, OS command orchestration, and domain operations in single packages, reducing responsibility clarity.
evidence: wifi.go stores shared runtime/process state (`WifiInfo`, `Device`, `ConfChannel`, locks, loop cancel func) while also implementing command execution and Wi-Fi operations.
system.go interleaves metrics collection, disk labeling, cron management, and command helper concerns in one file.
disk.go holds many mutable function seams as package globals and performs both filesystem orchestration and SMART/partition logic.
Tests need extensive seam-reset scaffolding (`resetDiskSeams`, custom runtime injectors) to isolate behavior, indicating tightly coupled responsibilities.
findings.suggestion: Split `system` into explicit components (state store, command executor, wifi service, disk service), inject dependencies via constructors rather than package globals, and expose narrow interfaces per responsibility so tests do not need global seam resets.

findings.summary: Config patch schema evolution relies on a large repetitive manual registry pattern that scales poorly and obscures intent.
evidence: config_update_schema.go defines a long `confPatchRegistry` with repeated per-field parse/has/apply closure triplets and pointer assignment boilerplate.
The registry pattern requires touching many repetitive blocks for each new field, increasing risk of mismatch between parse, presence checks, and apply behavior.
config_test.go carries very broad merge/config behavior tests, reflecting high surface complexity tied to this schema maintenance approach.
findings.suggestion: Move to a declarative schema map or generated field adapters (single source of truth per field for key/type/target path) and centralize parse/apply logic to reduce duplicated closure code and make new fields safer to add.

findings.summary: Repeated ad-hoc test harness patterns reveal missing shared fixtures and unstable state boundaries.
evidence: Importer tests define custom transition drain/read helpers and local state reset helpers; leak tests define separate reset/wait/must-receive helpers for similar channel/state orchestration.
Disk tests maintain a full manual backup/restore of package-level seams in `resetDiskSeams`, mirroring state-reset patterns in other modules.
These recurring local harness utilities indicate each package must manually repair mutable global state to test behavior.
findings.suggestion: Introduce shared test fixture builders per subsystem (import/leak/system) with standardized setup/teardown APIs and shift mutable globals behind injectable stores to reduce repetitive reset logic and improve test design coherence.
